title: Building Systematic Evaluations
description: Learn to design and implement rigorous evaluation suites for AI outputs — the foundation of reliable, production-grade AI systems that improve over time.
learningObjectives: Understand why systematic evals are essential for production AI systems
Design effective test cases that cover correctness, edge cases, and quality dimensions
Build an evaluation suite with scoring rubrics, automated checks, and regression tracking

sections.title: Why Evals Matter
sections.content:
In traditional software, you write tests that check deterministic behavior: given input X, expect output Y. AI systems are different — the same prompt can produce different outputs each time, and "correct" is often a spectrum rather than a binary. This makes evaluation both harder and more important.

**Evals are how you make progress measurable.** Without evals, you're optimizing prompts by vibes — you try a change, read a few outputs, and guess whether it's better. This doesn't scale, doesn't catch regressions, and doesn't survive team handoffs.

Systematic evals address this problem on several fronts. First, they give you **confidence to change prompts** — when you modify a system prompt, evals tell you whether the change helped, hurt, or had no effect across a representative set of inputs, not just the one example you happened to test. They also provide **regression detection**, because model updates, prompt changes, and system modifications can all cause subtle quality degradation, and evals catch this before your users do.

Just as importantly, evals give you **quantified quality**. Instead of saying "the outputs seem better," you can report that accuracy improved from 82% to 91% on your test suite with no regression on tone scores. Finally, an eval suite creates **shared understanding** across your team. It encodes what "good" means in concrete, measurable terms. A new engineer can look at the eval suite and immediately understand the quality bar they need to meet.

A useful mental model: **evals are to AI products what unit tests are to software, what clinical trials are to medicine, and what quality control is to manufacturing.** They're not optional for anything production-grade.

The biggest mistake teams make is treating evals as a one-time exercise. Evals should be a living artifact that grows with your product — new failure modes become new test cases, and eval scores are tracked over time like any other metric.

sections.title: Designing Effective Test Cases
sections.content:
A good eval suite has test cases that cover different dimensions of quality. Building one requires deliberate thought about what to test, how to measure it, and how to organize the results. Here's a framework for designing them.

**1. Start with failure modes, not happy paths**

You already know the easy cases work — those are the ones you tried while building the feature. The real value of an eval suite lies in systematically probing where things break. Think about edge cases like empty inputs, extremely long inputs, and unusual formats. Consider adversarial inputs such as prompt injections, contradictory instructions, and misleading context. Don't forget ambiguous inputs where the question is underspecified or has multiple valid interpretations, and domain boundaries where questions are almost but not quite in scope. These are the scenarios that will surprise you in production if you don't test for them upfront.

**2. Cover multiple quality dimensions**

A single score rarely captures everything that matters about an output. Instead, define your quality dimensions independently so you can see exactly where performance is strong and where it falls short. For example, consider how you might evaluate a response to "Explain quantum entanglement to a 10-year-old":
```
Test case: "Explain quantum entanglement to a 10-year-old"

Dimensions:
- Accuracy: Is the science correct? (1-5)
- Accessibility: Would a 10-year-old understand this? (1-5)
- Engagement: Is it interesting, not dry? (1-5)
- Conciseness: Is it the right length, not rambling? (1-5)
```

By scoring each dimension separately, you can distinguish between an answer that is scientifically accurate but too complex for the audience versus one that is age-appropriate but contains factual errors. This granularity is what makes evals actionable.

**3. Use a taxonomy of test types**

Different outputs call for different evaluation strategies. **Exact match** tests work well for structured outputs — checking whether the JSON has the right keys or whether a SQL query returns the correct rows. When the wording may vary but the meaning must be right, **semantic match** tests are more appropriate; Claude-as-judge works well for these. For open-ended tasks like writing, analysis, or explanation, **rubric-based** evaluation lets you score against multiple criteria simultaneously. **Comparative** tests ask whether output A is better than output B, which is especially useful when A/B testing prompt changes. And **safety/boundary** tests verify that the system correctly refuses out-of-scope requests and avoids harmful content.

Choosing the right test type for each case ensures you're measuring what actually matters rather than forcing a single evaluation approach onto every output.

**4. Annotate test cases with metadata**

Rich metadata transforms a flat list of test cases into a queryable dataset. By tagging each case with its category, difficulty, criticality, and expected traits, you can slice your results in powerful ways:
```json
{
  "input": "Summarize this legal contract in plain English",
  "context": "[contract text]",
  "category": "summarization",
  "difficulty": "hard",
  "critical": true,
  "expected_traits": ["mentions termination clause", "explains liability"],
  "forbidden_traits": ["legal jargon", "gives legal advice"]
}
```
With this structure in place, you can see accuracy broken down by category, by difficulty level, or filtered to just the critical cases that absolutely must pass. This makes it far easier to diagnose problems and prioritize fixes.

sections.title: Scoring and Automation
Build an Evaluation Suite
sections.content: Design a complete evaluation suite for a real AI feature.

exercise.prompt:
You're building an AI-powered customer support system that reads a customer's message and:
1. Classifies the intent (billing, technical, account, general)
2. Extracts key entities (order IDs, product names, dates)
3. Generates a helpful response

Design an evaluation suite for this system. Provide:

1. **Eight test cases** spanning different intents, edge cases, and difficulty levels. For each, specify the input message, expected intent classification, expected entities, and quality criteria for the response.

2. **Scoring approach** for each of the three outputs (classification, extraction, response). Specify which scoring method you'd use (code-based, Claude-as-judge, or both) and why.

3. **Three failure modes** you'd specifically test for, with example test cases.

4. **A composite scoring formula** that produces a single pass/fail for each test case, with clear thresholds.

Be specific — write out actual test case content, not just descriptions of what they would contain.
hints: Classification and extraction have objectively correct answers, so prefer code-based checks. Response quality is subjective, so use Claude-as-judge.
Failure modes to consider: multi-intent messages, messages with no clear intent, messages that try to manipulate the system, messages with misspelled entity names
Your composite score should weight hard failures (wrong classification) differently from soft failures (response could be more empathetic)
exercise.evaluationCriteria: Should provide: (1) Eight specific test cases with actual customer message text, not placeholders — covering standard cases (clear billing question), edge cases (message with two intents), adversarial cases (prompt injection attempt), ambiguous cases (complaint that could be billing or technical), and cases with tricky entity extraction (misspelled product names, multiple order IDs); (2) Scoring approach: exact match for classification, code-based extraction matching with fuzzy string comparison for entities, Claude-as-judge with a multi-dimensional rubric for response quality (helpfulness, accuracy, tone, completeness); (3) Three specific failure modes with example inputs — such as multi-intent handling, entity hallucination (extracting entities that aren't there), or unsafe response generation; (4) A composite formula with clear weights and thresholds — e.g., classification must be correct (hard gate), entity extraction F1 >= 0.8, response quality >= 3.5/5, with an overall pass requiring all three conditions.

sections.title: Key Takeaway
sections.content: Systematic evals transform AI development from guesswork into engineering. Design test cases around failure modes rather than happy paths, combine code-based checks with Claude-as-judge for comprehensive coverage, and track scores over time to catch regressions. An eval suite is a living artifact — every bug you find in production should become a new test case. The teams that ship reliable AI products are the ones that invest in evals early and run them on every change.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content: Reflect on the practical challenges of building and maintaining evals.

exercise.prompt:
Your team has been running an eval suite for three months. You have 50 test cases and use Claude-as-judge for scoring. You notice two problems:

1. The eval suite takes 20 minutes to run (because of 50 Claude API calls for judging), so developers skip running it before merging prompt changes.

2. On a subset of test cases, Claude-as-judge gives inconsistent scores — the same output gets a 3/5 on one run and a 5/5 on another.

How would you address each problem? What architectural changes or process changes would you make? Are there tradeoffs to your solutions?
exercise.evaluationCriteria: Should address both problems with practical solutions: (1) Speed problem — tier the eval suite: create a 'fast' subset of 10-15 critical test cases that runs in 3-4 minutes for pre-merge checks, run the full suite on CI after merge or nightly; use code-based checks wherever possible to replace slow Claude-as-judge calls; cache eval results for unchanged test cases; parallelize API calls. Tradeoff: fast suite might miss regressions that only appear in the full suite. (2) Consistency problem — reduce temperature to 0 for the judge calls; use more specific rubrics with concrete anchoring examples ('a score of 3 means X, a score of 5 means Y'); provide reference answers or few-shot examples in the judge prompt; run the judge multiple times and take the median score for high-variance cases; investigate whether the inconsistency is in specific test cases (suggesting the rubric is ambiguous for those cases). Tradeoff: stricter rubrics are more consistent but may miss nuanced quality differences; multiple judge runs increase cost and latency.
